did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a modified deep learning model based on the You Only Look Once (YOLO) algorithm, established on Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN), for the detection and classification of melanoma lesions from dermoscopic images. It also employs fuzzy logic for image segmentation and pre-processing to enhance lesion visibility.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a modified deep learning model (YOLO) combined with fuzzy logic for skin cancer detection. It details specific methods including image segmentation, pre-processing, and model architecture modifications, and reports new experimental results using the ISIC 2017, ISIC 2018, and PH2 datasets with performance metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

title: Fuzzy Logic with Deep Learning for Detection of Skin Cancer
brief_summary: This study proposes a modified deep learning model based on the You Only Look Once (YOLO) algorithm combined with fuzzy logic for the detection and segmentation of melanoma lesions from dermoscopic images. The method employs fuzzy logic-based image segmentation and pre-processing techniques, such as hair removal and histogram equalization, to enhance lesion visibility and remove artifacts. The model was trained on 10,695 dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2018 datasets and evaluated using the PH2 dataset as a holdout test set. Performance was assessed using metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, Jaccard Index, and Dice Coefficient. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed YOLO-based approach achieves superior accuracy and sensitivity compared to existing classifiers, with accuracy scores reaching up to 96.50% on the PH2 dataset. The authors conclude that this method effectively enhances early melanoma detection, with potential deployment via smartphone applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for detection and classification. The paper focuses on detecting malignant melanoma. In the PH2 dataset, 40 instances of melanoma lesions were used for testing. In ISIC datasets, melanoma cases were part of the training and testing sets (labeled as 'M' in Table 1).

Used as negative controls or comparators to distinguish from melanoma. The PH2 dataset included 160 instances of atypical and normal nevi for testing. The introduction mentions visual similarities between melanoma and benign lesions/moles. In ISIC datasets, non-melanoma cases were labeled as 'NM' in Table 1.

Mentioned in the Introduction as a commonly reported skin cancerous cell, but not explicitly listed as a specific target class in the experimental datasets or classification tasks described in the results.

Mentioned in the Introduction as a commonly reported skin cancerous cell, but not explicitly listed as a specific target class in the experimental datasets or classification tasks described in the results.

all_task_types.details: The paper uses a modified YOLO model to generate bounding boxes for the precise location detection of melanoma lesions in dermoscopic images. The model outputs bounding boxes based on confidence scores and class probabilities.

The YOLO classifier performs binary classification to identify malignant lesions (melanoma) versus non-melanoma lesions (benign nevi). Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and AUC.

A fuzzy logic-based method is used for image segmentation to enhance lesion visibility and generate precise segmented masks. This is a pre-processing step for detection but is also evaluated as a distinct task using Jaccard Index and Dice Coefficient.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2017 challenge, containing dermoscopic lesion images annotated by expert dermatologists with ground truth values for classification and segmentation. Sourced from the ISIC library and HAM10000 dataset.

use.training: Used for training the classifier (2000 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed algorithm (600 images).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 challenge, containing dermoscopic lesion images annotated by expert dermatologists with ground truth values for classification and segmentation. Sourced from the ISIC library and HAM10000 dataset.

use.training: Used for training the classifier (8695 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed algorithm (1320 images).

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset published by the dermatology department of Pedro-Hispano hospital, containing dermoscopic lesion images composed by specialized dermatologists. Images are 768x560 pixels in resolution, captured with 20x magnification.

use.testing: Used as a holdout dataset for testing and evaluating the proposed method.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, IoU, Dice Coefficient, and Jaccard Index.

PH2.notes: Used as a holdout dataset for testing. The proposed method was compared against existing approaches and other classifiers.

ISIC 2017.notes: Used for training and testing. The proposed method was compared against existing approaches.

ISIC 2018.notes: Used for training and testing. The proposed method was compared against existing approaches.

architecture_used.main_model: Modified You Only Look Once (YOLO) algorithm based on Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN)

modifications: Increased depth by adding more convolutional layers
Introduced feature concatenation at different layers to combine multi-scale features
architecture_details.final_output_shape: 7 x 7 x 30 (indicating two boundary boxes for each location)

training_details.optimizer: Stochastic Gradient Descent (implied by momentum/decay parameters)

classification_or_output_head.task: Binary classification (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma) and object detection (bounding boxes)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Modified You Only Look Once (YOLO) algorithm
Multi-staged fully convolutional network (FCN) with parallel integration (mFCN-PI)
Synchronized segmentation with classification methods using a bootstrapping convolutional neural network model
Fully convolutional network (FCN) architecture with ResNet18 and AlexNet
preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using the Dull Razor algorithm with gray-morphological operation to identify hair location and bilinear interpolation to restore affected pixels
Application of an adaptive-median filter to smoothen the image after hair removal
Histogram equalization to adjust contrast and brightness for image enhancement
Resizing images to 512 x 512 pixels for classifier training

data_augmentation_techniques.details: All dermoscopic images with varying resolutions were resized to 512 x 512 pixels before undergoing the training process to reduce computational usage.

The authors state an aim to use generative adversarial networks (GAN) for high-quality synthetic data generation to increase the size of the dataset and improve classification accuracy, but this was not implemented in the current study due to limited data availability.

conclusions: The proposed method, which combines fuzzy logic-based image segmentation with a modified YOLO deep learning model, effectively enhances the visibility of melanoma lesions by removing artifacts such as hair follicles and dermoscopic scales.

The integration of L-R fuzzy defuzzification and probabilistic thresholding in the segmentation process significantly improves accuracy, increasing the average score from approximately 95% in the first phase to 96% in the second phase.

The modified YOLO model, enhanced with increased depth, residual connections, and multi-scale feature concatenation, achieves superior detection performance compared to existing classifiers, with high sensitivity and specificity scores across ISIC 2017, ISIC 2018, and PH2 datasets.
On the PH2 dataset, the proposed method achieved a sensitivity of 95%, specificity of 96.25%, and an Intersection over Union (IoU) of 94% for lesion detection.

In the second phase of segmentation on the PH2 dataset, the method achieved an accuracy of 96.50%, sensitivity of 97.50%, specificity of 96.25%, Jaccard Index of 84.78%, and Dice Coefficient of 91.76%.
The proposed YOLO-based classifier demonstrated high classification performance on the PH2 dataset with an accuracy of 98.16%, sensitivity of 95.43%, specificity of 99.50%, and an AUC of 0.99.

The study concludes that the proposed approach is faster and more accurate than most pre-existing classifiers, supporting its potential for deployment in smartphone applications for early melanoma detection.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a limitation of using a limited dataset size, specifically noting that the availability of limited data from ISIC 2017 and 2018 constrained the training set.
The authors admit that the current pre-processing algorithm requires enhancement to more effectively remove digital artifacts.

The paper plans to incorporate Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) for synthetic data generation in future work to address data limitations, indicating this was not implemented in the current study.

The authors note a need to add clinical feature extraction methods (based on the ABCD rule) to decrease specificity and better assure medical practitioners, suggesting current specificity may be suboptimal for clinical trust.

The classifier was trained by solely resizing images to 512x512 pixels while modifying only the last layer of the YOLO classifier, which may limit the depth of feature learning compared to full fine-tuning.

The PH2 dataset was used exclusively as a holdout test set, while ISIC 2017 and 2018 were used for both training and testing, which may introduce bias if the distribution of these datasets differs significantly.
The research received no external funding, which may be a consideration for assessing potential conflicts of interest or resource constraints.
